title: Math for AI
description: Enter the Math Forge and learn the practical, visual math AI uses to measure, compare, and improve.
outcomes: Explain why AI systems need math without turning it into school math.
Describe vectors, matrices, dot products, probability, statistics, gradients, and gradient descent in plain English.
Use tiny Python examples to represent similarity, uncertainty, summaries, and improvement direction.
Connect math ideas to embeddings, recommendations, classifiers, and model training.
Build a practical Math Forge report that explains an AI system using visual mental models.

bossBattle.title: Mini Project: Boss Battle - Math Forge
bossBattle.description: Prove you can connect vectors, matrices, similarity, probability, statistics, and gradients.

curatedResources.title: 3Blue1Brown Neural Networks / Deep Learning Visual Series
curatedResources.placement: Use for visual intuition across vectors, matrices, and similarity.
curatedResources.why: Watch this when abstract math concepts feel confusing; the visuals make structure and direction clear.
curatedResources.whenToUse: Use in Math Forge when introducing vectors, matrices, dot products, and gradients.

lessons.title: Quest: Why Math Matters in AI
lessons.description: See math as the hidden language AI uses to measure the world.
checklist: I can explain why AI needs numbers.
I can connect math to measurement, comparison, and improvement.
I can name one AI feature where math appears under the hood.
questions: Why does AI need to turn real-world things into numbers?
What is one place math appears in an AI product?

curatedResources.title: 3Blue1Brown Neural Networks / Deep Learning Visual Series
curatedResources.placement: Use when learning why AI uses numerical representations.
curatedResources.why: The series helps connect geometric intuition to practical AI representation choices.
curatedResources.whenToUse: Before moving from intuition to vectors in lesson 2.

lessons.title: Quest: Vectors in Plain English
lessons.description: Understand vectors as lists of numbers that represent meaning or direction.
checklist: I can define a vector in plain English.
I can explain how vectors can represent meaning.
I can create a tiny vector in Python.
questions: What does a vector represent in an AI system?
Why are embeddings often stored as vectors?

curatedResources.title: 3Blue1Brown Neural Networks / Deep Learning Visual Series
curatedResources.placement: Use before writing first vector math examples.
curatedResources.why: This resource builds geometric intuition for vectors and embeddings.
curatedResources.whenToUse: Review before comparing vector examples and similarity concepts.

lessons.title: Quest: Matrices in Plain English
lessons.description: Use matrices as tables of numbers AI uses for computation.
checklist: I can define a matrix as a table of numbers.
I can identify rows, columns, and shape.
I can explain why shape matters in AI code.
questions: How is a matrix different from a vector?
Why does matrix shape matter when working with model inputs?

curatedResources.title: 3Blue1Brown Neural Networks / Deep Learning Visual Series
curatedResources.placement: Use before moving from vectors to matrix-style batching and transformations.
curatedResources.why: The visuals help connect matrices to AI’s multi-vector computation workflows.
curatedResources.whenToUse: Review before later matrix examples in embeddings and retrieval.

lessons.title: Quest: Dot Products and Similarity
lessons.description: Use dot products as a practical way to compare direction and similarity.
checklist: I can explain dot product as a similarity signal.
I can calculate a tiny dot product.
I can connect dot products to search and recommendations.
questions: Why does direction matter when comparing two vectors?
Where does similarity scoring appear in AI products?

curatedResources.title: 3Blue1Brown Neural Networks / Deep Learning Visual Series
curatedResources.placement: Use before applying dot products to recommendations or retrieval tasks.
curatedResources.why: The visuals explain overlap and directional alignment clearly.
curatedResources.whenToUse: Use before completing similarity drills in this module.

lessons.title: Quest: Probability for AI
lessons.description: Treat probability as how likely something is, not as abstract formulas.
checklist: I can explain probability as likelihood.
I can read simple confidence scores carefully.
I can name one risk of treating probability as certainty.
questions: Why is probability useful for AI predictions?
What can go wrong when a probability score is treated as a guarantee?

lessons.title: Quest: Statistics for AI
lessons.description: Summarize data with practical statistics that help you understand inputs.
checklist: I can explain mean, median, spread, and outliers.
I can summarize a small dataset in Python.
I can connect statistics to data quality.
questions: How do summary statistics help before training a model?
Why can an average hide important data problems?

lessons.title: Quest: Derivatives and Gradients
lessons.description: Understand gradients as direction of improvement.
checklist: I can define a gradient in plain English.
I can connect slope to improvement direction.
I can explain why models need direction, not just a final answer.
questions: What does a gradient tell a model during training?
Why is direction useful when trying to reduce errors?

curatedResources.title: 3Blue1Brown Neural Networks / Deep Learning Visual Series
curatedResources.placement: Use before your first optimization intuition pass.
curatedResources.why: Great visual explanation of direction and optimization flow.

lessons.title: Quest: Gradient Descent
lessons.description: See gradient descent as the step-by-step process models use to reduce mistakes.
checklist: I can explain gradient descent without formulas.
I can describe learning rate as step size.
I can identify why too-large or too-small steps are a problem.
questions: How does gradient descent help a model improve?
Why does step size matter?

lessons.title: Portfolio Project: Math Forge Field Guide
lessons.description: Create a beginner-friendly visual guide that explains the math behind one AI feature.
checklist: I can explain an AI feature using practical math concepts.
I can include one small code example.
I can make the explanation visual and beginner-friendly.
questions: Which AI feature did you explain?
Which math concepts did your guide use?

lessons.title: Mini Project: Boss Battle - Math Forge
lessons.description: Prove you can connect vectors, matrices, similarity, probability, statistics, and gradients.
checklist: I can solve the Boss Battle prompts in plain English.
I can justify which math idea fits each AI scenario.
I can explain my answers without relying on textbook language.
questions: Which math concept felt most useful for AI?
Which concept needs another pass before machine learning?

lessons.title: Knowledge Trial: Math for AI
lessons.description: Check that you can explain practical AI math in your own words.
checklist: I can explain vectors, matrices, similarity, probability, statistics, and gradients.
I can connect math ideas to AI product behavior.
I can move into Data for AI with practical confidence.
questions: How do vectors and dot products work together in AI search?
How does gradient descent reduce model mistakes?

quizQuestions.prompt: Explain how vectors can represent meaning in an AI system.
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: A vector is a list of numbers that represents features, meaning, or direction. AI systems use vectors such as embeddings so similar items can be compared mathematically.

rubric.label: Connects vectors to meaning or features

quizQuestions.prompt: Why is a dot product useful when comparing two AI vectors?
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: A dot product combines matching positions in two vectors to produce a similarity signal, so AI systems can rank items that point in a similar direction.

rubric.label: Connects to search, ranking, or recommendation

quizQuestions.prompt: Explain gradient descent in plain English.
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: Gradient descent is a step-by-step way for a model to reduce mistakes by moving in the direction that lowers error, using a learning rate as the step size.

rubric.label: Says the model reduces error or mistakes
Mentions step size or learning rate
